title: Tree Path Sum

content.problemStatement:
Given an undirected connected tree with **N** nodes, numbered from **1** to **N**, and rooted at node **1**, along with an integer `targetSum`, check if there exists a path from root to any leaf whose sum is equal to `targetSum`.

For example, if `targetSum` is 9, then in the following tree, there is a path **1 -> 2 -> 6** whose sum is 9.




Input Format

The first line of the input contains two space separated integer NNN and `targetSum` — the number of vertices and the target sum which needs to be checked.
The next N−1N - 1N−1 lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these N−1N - 1N−1 lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting a bidirectional edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.


Output Format
Output **YES** if tree has root to leaf path sum equal to given `targetSum` or **NO** otherwise.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and targetSum. The next N‑1 lines contain two integers u and v each, denoting an undirected edge between nodes u and v. The tree is rooted at node 1.
content.outputFormat: For each test case print a single line containing YES if there exists a root‑to‑leaf path whose sum of node indices equals targetSum, otherwise print NO.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Leaf paths are 1‑2‑5 (sum 8), 1‑2‑3 (6), 1‑2‑6 (9) and 1‑4‑7 (12). One leaf path matches the target, so answer is YES.
Leaf paths sums are 1‑2‑4 = 7, 1‑2‑5 = 8, 1‑3 = 4. None equals 10, therefore NO.
Leaf paths are 1‑2 (sum 3) and 1‑3 (sum 4). No leaf path matches the target, so answer is NO.

solution.approach: The tree is traversed from the root using a depth‑first search. While descending we keep the running sum of node indices. When a leaf (node with degree 1 except the root) is reached we compare the accumulated sum with targetSum. If any leaf matches, the answer is YES. The DFS visits each edge once, giving O(N) time per test case and O(N) memory for the adjacency list.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for adjacency lists and auxiliary arrays.
